questions.question: Explain the five key objectives of distributed systems. Why is each objective important?
keyPoints: Resource Sharing: Enables sharing of expensive hardware, software, and data across the system
Openness: Allows system to be extended with new components and services
Concurrency: Enables multiple users to access shared resources simultaneously
Scalability: Allows system to handle increased load by adding resources
Fault Tolerance: Ensures system continues functioning despite component failures

questions.question: Define transparency in distributed systems and list the different types of transparency. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Transparency: Hides distribution complexity from users
Types: Access, Location, Migration, Replication, Concurrency, Failure transparency
Example Location: Using logical names instead of physical IP addresses
Example Replication: Multiple database copies appear as single database
Example Failure: System automatically redirects requests when a server fails

questions.question: Differentiate between horizontal and vertical scalability. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Horizontal: Adding more machines/nodes to the system
Vertical: Adding more resources (CPU, RAM) to existing machines
Horizontal preferred: When cloud infrastructure is available, need better fault tolerance
Vertical preferred: When application is hard to partition, when single-machine performance is critical
Trade-offs: Horizontal offers better fault tolerance but adds complexity; Vertical simpler but has single point of failure

questions.question: What is middleware? Explain its role in addressing heterogeneity in distributed systems.
keyPoints: Middleware: Software layer between OS and applications
Role in heterogeneity: Provides abstraction over different hardware, OS, networks
Examples: CORBA, DCOM, RMI, gRPC
Services: Communication, transaction management, security, naming
Enables: Heterogeneous systems to communicate without worrying about底层 differences

questions.question: State and explain the CAP theorem. Why is it important for distributed system design?
keyPoints: CAP Theorem: A distributed system can guarantee only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
During partition: Must choose between Consistency and Availability
Important: Guides design decisions based on application requirements
Examples: CP systems (databases like HBase), AP systems (like Cassandra)
Trade-off: System cannot be both consistent and available during network partitions

questions.question: List and explain the main challenges in designing distributed systems.
keyPoints: Heterogeneity: Different hardware, OS, networks must work together
Security: Multiple entry points increase vulnerability
Scalability: Maintaining performance as system grows
Concurrency: Managing simultaneous access to resources
Fault Tolerance: Detecting, masking, recovering from failures
Transparency: Hiding complexity while maintaining usability
Openness: Supporting extensions and interoperability

questions.question: Explain the client-server model with a diagram. What are its advantages and limitations?
keyPoints: Model: Client requests services, server provides services
Advantages: Simple, centralized resources, easy to manage, scalable
Limitations: Single point of failure, server bottleneck, network dependency
Examples: Web applications, database systems, file servers
Variants: Thin client, thick client, three-tier architecture

questions.question: A company wants to build a distributed e-commerce platform that serves customers globally. Discuss the design considerations they must address, focusing on: (a) Meeting the five objectives of distributed systems (b) Addressing the major challenges (c) Choosing appropriate architecture patterns
keyPoints: Objectives: Resource sharing (products, inventory), Openness (APIs for integration), Concurrency (multiple buyers), Scalability (peak traffic), Fault tolerance (24/7 availability)
Challenges: Heterogeneity (global infrastructure), Security (payment data), Scalability (flash sales), Concurrency (inventory management), Transparency (location-aware content)
Architecture: Consider microservices, CDN for content, database replication, load balancing, caching layers
Trade-offs: CAP theorem implications for inventory vs. availability
Technology choices: Cloud-native, containerization, distributed databases

questions.question: Explain what fault tolerance means in distributed systems. How is it achieved? Discuss different techniques used for failure detection, masking, and recovery with examples.
keyPoints: Fault Tolerance: System continues functioning despite failures
Failure Detection: Heartbeats (servers send periodic signals), timeouts (request response expected within time), checksums (data corruption detection)
Failure Masking: Retransmission (retry failed operations), redundancy (duplicate components), error-correcting codes
Failure Recovery: Checkpointing (save state periodically), state replication (maintain copies), failover (switch to backup)
Examples: RAID (disk redundancy), Primary-backup replication, Paxos/Raft consensus
Trade-offs: Cost vs. reliability, performance overhead of redundancy

questions.question: Compare and contrast peer-to-peer and client-server architectures in distributed systems. Discuss scenarios where each would be more appropriate.
keyPoints: Client-Server: Centralized server provides services, clients request services. Examples: Web apps, databases. Pros: Manageable, secure. Cons: Single point of failure, scalability limits
Peer-to-Peer: All nodes equal, can be both client and server. Examples: BitTorrent, blockchain. Pros: No central bottleneck, resilient, scalable. Cons: Complex, security challenges
When to use Client-Server: Enterprise applications, when security is critical, when centralized control needed
When to use P2P: File sharing, decentralized applications, when no central authority exists
Hybrid approaches: Some systems combine both (e.g., Skype initially P2P, later server-assisted)
